It has been said that (*what they desire*) is a delayed subject (مبتدأ مؤخر) and (*for them*) is a preceding predicate (خبر مقدم), and the sentence is coordinated with (*they assign*). According to this, the sentence is intended for rebuke or mockery.

They justified this by saying that coordinating the sentence with (*daughters to Allah*) is not permissible because it violates the rule: that a transitive verb which reaches its object directly or via a preposition, if its subject is an attached nominative pronoun, cannot reach that same pronoun directly or via a preposition except with a separator. For example, if Zayd struck himself, one does not say: *Zayd struck him* or *You struck you*; and if he was angry with himself, one does not say: *Zayd was angry with him*. Rather, it is said: *Zayd struck himself* or *He struck none but him*, and *Zayd was angry with himself* or *He was angry with none but him* — except in the category of verbs of "supposition" (ظن) and what is attached to them like "loss" and "absence," where it is permissible to say: *Zayd thought him strong* (meaning himself).

Based on this, if His saying: (*while for themselves they have what they desire*) were coordinated with His saying: (*daughters to Allah*), it would have been mandatory to say: "and for their own selves (لأنفسهم) what they desire." (End of summary).

The truth is that adherence to this rule is only to prevent ambiguity (اللبس), and the intervention of a preposition between the two pronouns constitutes a separation. In the Noble Quran: **وَهُزِّي إِلَيْكِ بِجِذْعِ النَّخْلَةِ** — "Shake the trunk of the palm tree toward yourself" [19:25], and **وَاضْمُمْ إِلَيْكَ جَناحَكَ** — "And draw your hand to your side" [28:32]. Some have rejected the rule entirely because it is invalidated by these two verses. They also answered with other points that we have no need to mention; whoever desires them should refer to the commentaries.

**His saying, the Exalted**: (*When one of them is brought the news of a female [newborn], his face becomes darkened and he is choked with grief*) [16:58]. The darkening of the face is a metaphor for anger, and the "choked with grief" (الكظيم) is one who swallows his rage. The sentence is a circumstantial clause (حالية); that is, they attribute daughters to their Lord while the state is such that if one of them is given the news of a female — and it is said to him: "A daughter has been born to you" — his face turns black with rage while he swallows his anger.

**His saying, the Exalted**: (*He hides from the people because of the evil of the news he has been given...*) [16:59] to the end of the verse. Hiding (التواري) is to conceal oneself, derived from "behind" (الوراء). Ignominy (الهون) is humiliation and disgrace, and burying (الدس) is concealment.

The meaning: This person who has been given the news of a daughter hides from the people because of the "evil" of the news he has received according to his belief. He ponders his affair: shall he keep what he has been given news of — which is the daughter — despite the humiliation of keeping and protecting her, or shall he hide her in the dust? This was their custom regarding female newborns, as it is said that one of them would dig a small pit, and if the newborn was female, he would place her in the pit and heap dust upon her until she died beneath it. They used to do this for fear of poverty for them, or that those who were not their equals would desire them.

The first time this appeared among them was when the tribe of Bani Tamim raided Khosrow, who defeated them and took their women and children captive. He brought them into the royal palace and took the girls as maidservants and concubines. After a while, they made peace and sought the return of the captives. The girls were given the choice to return to their families, but several of them refused. This enraged the men of Bani Tamim, so they resolved that no female would be born to them except that they would bury her alive (وأدوها). Then some of those below them followed them in this, and the practice of burying daughters alive (وأد البنات) became widespread among them.

His saying: (*Behold! Evil is the judgment they make*) refers to their judgment that He has daughters while they have sons — not because of the inherent lowliness of daughters or the nobility of sons in reality, but rather the meaning of this judgment in their view is that Allah should have what they dislike while they have what they love. It has been said: what is meant by the judgment is their judgment regarding the obligation of burying daughters alive and that keeping them is an ignominy. The first of the two views is more fitting and appropriate for the following verse.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **لِلَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ مَثَلُ السَّوْءِ وَلِلَّهِ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** (For those who do not believe in the Hereafter is an evil attribute, and to Allah belongs the highest attribute, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. 16:60). The attribute (المثل) refers to a quality or description (الصفة); from this, a popular proverb (المثل السائر) is so named because it is a description that circulates upon tongues and is applied in every situation that suits or resembles it.

The word "evil" (السوء) — *with a fatḥah on the sīn and a sukūn on the wāw* — is the verbal noun (مصدر) of "to be bad" (ساء يسوء), just as "evil" (السُّوء) — *with a ḍammah* — is its noun. The annexation of "attribute" to "evil" serves to categorize, for things are described either from the perspective of their goodness (حسن) or from the perspective of their evil and ugliness (قبح). Thus, attributes are of two types: the good attribute and the evil attribute.

Goodness and ugliness may sometimes stem from innate creation (الخلقة), where a human has no hand or choice, such as beauty of face or deformity of build. At other times, they attach to a person due to voluntary actions (الأعمال الاختيارية), such as the goodness of justice and the ugliness of oppression. Reason (العقل) only praises or blames that which belongs to the second category, not the first. Therefore, praise and blame essentially revolve around acting according to what the human primordial nature (الفطرة الإنسانية) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — deems good and commands. This includes actions that lead a person to the happiness of his life and the abandonment of their opposites, which is what the true religion (الدين الحق) contains of primordial laws.

It is well known that the human temperament (الطبع) has no deterrent against committing an evil act except the pain of accountability and the severity of punishment, and the person's submission to the fact that such punishment will occur and be fulfilled. As for blame, it turns into praise if the act becomes widespread and ceases to be considered a reprehensible (منكر) deed.

From here, it becomes clear that belief in the Hereafter (الإيمان بالآخرة) and submission to the Reckoning (الحساب) and Requital (الجزاء) is the sole foundation that guarantees safeguarding a human from committing evil deeds and protects him from the attachment of any blame or disgrace. It is the source that rectifies human actions in a way that compels him to adhere to the path of happiness. No other foundational knowledge (المعارف الأصلية) can produce this effect, not even monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — to which every other principle ultimately returns.

To this, His saying, the Exalted, points: **وَلا تَتَّبِعِ الْهَوى فَيُضِلَّكَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ** (and do not follow desire, or it will lead you astray from the way of Allah. Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because of their forgetting the Day of Reckoning. 38:26).

Thus, the lack of belief in the Hereafter and the belittling of the matter of Reckoning and Requital is the source and destination of every evil deed. Conversely, belief in the Hereafter is the origin of every good deed and the fountainhead of every blessing. Every evil attribute (مثل سوء) and quality of ugliness that attaches to a human only comes to him by way of forgetting the Hereafter, just as every good attribute and quality of praise is the opposite.

Through what has preceded, the subtle point (النكتة) in His saying: **لِلَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ مَثَلُ السَّوْءِ** (For those who do not believe in the Hereafter is an evil attribute) becomes apparent. He had been describing them in the previous verses with polytheism (الشرك), but when He intended to explain that they possess the evil attribute, He substituted that with the description of their lack of belief in the Hereafter.

Those who do not believe in the Hereafter are the primary source for the occurrence of every evil attribute and quality of ugliness, for its criterion—which is the denial of the Hereafter—is an inseparable trait of theirs. If some of those who believe in the Hereafter are afflicted by an evil attribute, it only attaches to them due to forgetting the Day of Reckoning, while the deniers are the root of that.

This concerns evil qualities that reason deems ugly and blameworthy. However, there are other "evil" attributes that reason does not deem ugly but which the temperament (الطبع) dislikes, such as femininity among some people, the birth of daughters among others, financial poverty, illness, death, extinction (الفناء), incapacity (العجز), and ignorance (الجهل). These are shared between the believer and the disbeliever. There are also other analytical attributes such as poverty, need, deficiency, non-existence (العدم), and contingency (الإمكان) — *the state of being that requires a cause to exist* — which are not exclusive to humans but are shared among all contingent beings (الممكنات) and permeate all creation. The disbeliever is characterized by them just as others are. Thus, the disbeliever is subject to being described by every evil attribute—some specific to him and some shared between him and others, as explained in detail.

Allah, the Glorified, is transcendent (منزه) above being described by any of these qualities which are evil attributes (أمثال السوء). As for the evil attributes resulting from evil deeds—which reason deems ugly and blameworthy, and which are summarized by oppression (الظلم)—He does not oppress anything. The Exalted said: **وَلا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَداً** (and your Lord does not wrong anyone. 18:49) and said: **وَهُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْعَلِيمُ** (and He is the All-wise, the All-knowing. 43:84). Whatever decree He ordains or act He performs is the very requirement of wisdom (الحكمة); nothing else would be appropriate regarding the prevailing system of existence.

As for the evil attributes that the temperament dislikes or reason analytically identifies, there is no way for them to reach Him, the Exalted. For He is the Absolute Mighty (عزيز مطلق) whose side is immune to the infiltration of any abasement (ذلة). He possesses all power (القدرة), so no incapacity (عجز) affects Him; He possesses all knowledge (العلم), so no ignorance (جهل) occurs to Him; and He possesses pure life (الحياة), so neither death nor extinction threatens Him. He is transcendent above every deficiency and non-existence, so He is not described by the qualities of bodies (الأجسام) which involve deficiency, loss, inadequacy, or frailty. The verses regarding these meanings are numerous and clear, requiring no citation.

He, the Glorified, possesses such loftiness and purity that He is not described by any of the evil attributes that characterize others. Not only is He transcendent to this degree, but He is also transcendent above being described by any of the good attributes (الأمثال الحسنة) and noble, beautiful qualities in the sense that they characterize others—such as life, knowledge, power, might, greatness, and majesty. For the good, perfect qualities found in contingent beings are limited, finite, tainted by poverty and need, and mixed with loss and deficiency. However, the attributes belonging to Him, the Glorified, are pure perfection (محض الكمال), and their reality is unlimited, infinite, and untainted by deficiency or non-existence. He has a life not threatened by death, a power not touched by fatigue or incapacity, a knowledge not accompanied by ignorance, and a might with which there is no abasement.

Thus, to Him belongs the highest attribute (المثل الأعلى) and the most beautiful quality. The Exalted said: **وَلَهُ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (To Him belongs the highest attribute in the heavens and the earth. 30:27) and said: **لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى** (To Him belong the most beautiful names. 20:8). Attributes (الأمثال) include the lowly and the lofty; and among the lofty, some are "highest" (أعلى) and some are not. The "highest" is His attribute, the Exalted. Names (الأسماء) include the evil and the good; and among the good, some are "most beautiful" (أحسن) and some are not. To Allah belongs that which is most beautiful. Understand this.

It has become clear from the preceding the meaning of His attribute being "highest," and that His saying: **وَلِلَّهِ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى** (and to Allah belongs the highest attribute) is phrased for exclusivity (الحصر). That is, to Allah alone belongs the attribute that is highest, to the exclusion of the attribute that is evil and lowly, and to the exclusion of the attribute that is good and lofty—referring to the qualities of perfection that characterize contingent beings but are not "highest."

It has also become clear that the "highest attribute" that appears for Him, the Exalted, from the previous explanation, is the negation of all evil qualities from Him, as He said: **لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ** (Nothing is like Him. 42:11), and from the affirmative qualities (الصفات الثبوتية), every good quality with its limits and deficiencies negated.

His saying: **وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** (and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise) is phrased to provide exclusivity and to justify what preceded it. That is, He is the One who possesses all might (العزة), so no abasement touches Him at all, because every abasement is the loss of some might, and He does not lack any might. He possesses all wisdom (الحكمة), so no ignorance affects Him, because ignorance is the loss of some wisdom, and He lacks nothing of wisdom.

Since there is no way for abasement or ignorance to reach Him, He is not described by any qualities of deficiency, nor is He characterized by any traits of blame or evil attributes. However, the disbeliever is abased in his essence and ignorant in himself; thus, qualities of deficiency attach to and accompany him, and he is described by traits of blame and evil attributes. Therefore, "for those who do not believe in the Hereafter is an evil attribute."

The believer, although abased in his essence and ignorant in himself like the disbeliever, has entered into the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah. Thus, his Lord has granted him might through His might and made him prevail over ignorance by supporting him with a spirit from Him. The Exalted said: **وَاللهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (and Allah is the Guardian of the believers. 3:68), and said: **وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (and all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the believers. 63:8), and said: **أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ** (He has written faith into their hearts and strengthened them with a spirit from Him. 58:22).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَوْ يُؤاخِذُ اللهُ النَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِمْ ما تَرَكَ عَلَيْها مِنْ دَابَّةٍ** (Were Allah to take mankind to task for their wrongdoing, He would not leave any living being upon it... 16:61) to the end of the verse. The pronoun in "upon it" (عَلَيْها) refers to the earth, as "mankind" (النَّاسَ) indicates it.

It is not far-fetched to claim that the context indicates that "living being" (دابة) refers only to the human being, from the perspective of his crawling and moving (يدب ويتحرك). The meaning would be: If Allah were to take mankind to task for their wrongdoing, continuing the accountability, He would not leave on the earth any human who crawls and moves. As for the majority of people, they would perish due to their wrongdoing. As for the rarest few—the Prophets and the Imams who are infallible (المعصومون) from wrongdoing—they would not exist due to the prior perishing of their fathers and mothers.

The commentators have taken "living being" (الدابة) in the verse in its general sense, which is everything that crawls upon the earth of humans and animals. Thus, the meaning of the verse becomes that if He were to take them to task for their wrongdoing, He would destroy mankind and every animal on earth. This raises the question: If it is the human who perishes due to his wrongdoing, why should the rest of the animals perish when they have no wrongdoing, or do they perish due to the wrongdoing of the human?

The most plausible answer given is the statement of some, with our refinement: If Allah were to take them to task for their wrongdoing—whether disbelief or disobedience—the general population would perish due to their wrongdoing except the infallible ones. As for the infallible ones, despite their rarity and small number, they would not exist due to the prior perishing of their fathers and mothers. When humans perish and procreation ceases, the "living beings" of other animals would perish because they were created for the benefits and interests of the servants, as suggested by His saying, the Exalted: **خَلَقَ لَكُمْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً** (It is He who created for you all that is in the earth. 2:29).

They have other views in defending the verse on the assumption that "living being" is general, but there is no benefit in recounting them. Whoever desires them should refer to the lengthy commentaries.

Some have used the verse to argue against the infallibility (عصمة) of the Prophets (peace be upon them). The response is that the verse does not indicate more than that if Allah were to take people to task for wrongdoing, all people would perish and the species would become extinct. However, it does not indicate that everyone who perishes does so only because of his own wrongdoing. It is possible that the majority perish due to their wrongdoing, and the minority perish due to the extinction of their fathers and mothers, as mentioned before. Thus, the verse does not indicate that wrongdoing encompasses every individual, including the Prophets and the infallible ones; it only indicates that the extinction encompasses everyone.

It has been said in response that "mankind" refers to the wrongdoers among them, by the evidence of His saying: "for their wrongdoing" (بِظُلْمِهِمْ), so it does not include the infallible ones from the start.

It has also been answered that "wrongdoing" (الظلم) is more general than disobedience (المعصية)—which is the violation of a masterly command—and includes "leaving the better option" (ترك الأولى), which is the violation of an advisory command. This sometimes occurred from the Prophets (peace be upon them), as narrated from Adam and his wife: **قالا رَبَّنا ظَلَمْنا أَنْفُسَنا** (They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves.’ 7:23) and other Prophets. Thus, the good deeds of the righteous are the "sins" of those drawn near (المقربين). In this case, the generality of "wrongdoing" in the verse including the Prophets does not indicate a lack of their infallibility from disobedience in the sense of violating a masterly command.

It has also been answered that the destruction of all people would occur by Allah withholding the descent of rain upon the earth due to the wrongdoing of the wrongdoers among mankind. Thus, the wrongdoers, the saints, and the animals would all perish. For when punishment descends, it does not differentiate between the wretched and the felicitous; it would be a vengeance and exemplary punishment for the enemy, and a trial and increase in reward for others.

All three answers are incomplete. As for the first: restricting "mankind" to the wrongdoers would necessitate restricting the destruction to them, as claimed. Thus, the destruction would not encompass the infallible ones, and there would be no reason then for the destruction of the rest of the living beings created for the human. Therefore, His saying: "He would not leave any living being upon it" would not be sound, as is not hidden.

As for the second: the verses, given their context, discuss wrongdoing in the sense of polytheism and other masterly disobediences. Generalizing "wrongdoing" in the verse to include "leaving the better option"—especially considering the end of the verse: **وَلكِنْ يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** (but He respites them until a specified time)—which clearly implies a threat, does not suit the context.

As for the third: there is no evidence from the wording for what was mentioned in it.

His saying: **وَلكِنْ يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَلا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ** (but He respites them until a specified time. When their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it. 16:61) is an emendation (استدراك) from a suppressed premise indicated by the conditional clause at the beginning of the verse. The implied meaning is: He does not hasten in taking them to task, but He delays them until a specified time (أجل مسمى). The "specified time" regarding an individual human is his inevitable death; regarding a nation (الأمة), it is the day of its extinction; and regarding mankind in general, it is the blowing of the Trumpet and the rising of the Hour. Each of these has a mention in His speech, the Exalted. He said: **وَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُتَوَفَّى مِنْ قَبْلُ وَلِتَبْلُغُوا أَجَلاً مُسَمًّى** (and some of you are taken away before, and that you may complete a specified term. 40:67); and He said: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَلا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ** (There is a time for every nation: when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it. 7:34); and He said: **وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ** (Had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord, the matter would have been decided between them until a specified time. 42:14).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ ما يَكْرَهُون وتَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُهُمُ الْكَذِبَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ الْحُسْنى** (They ascribe to Allah what they [themselves] dislike, and their tongues utter the lie that the best of things is for them... 16:62) to the end of the verse. This is a return to the polytheists' attribution of daughters to Him, the Exalted, while choosing sons for themselves, though they dislike daughters and love and prefer sons.

Thus, His saying: **وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ ما يَكْرَهُونَ** (They ascribe to Allah what they dislike) means daughters. His saying: **وَتَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُهُمُ الْكَذِبَ** (and their tongues utter the lie) means their tongues report the false news, which is: **أَنَّ لَهُمُ الْحُسْنى** (that the best of things is for them), meaning the good outcome of life, which is that sons should succeed them. It has been said that "the best" (الحسنى) refers to Paradise, on the assumption of the truth of the Resurrection and the truthfulness of the Prophets in what they report, as He narrated from them in His saying: **وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هذا لِي وَما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً وَلَئِنْ رُجِعْتُ إِلى رَبِّي إِنَّ لِي عِنْدَهُ لَلْحُسْنى** (And if We let him taste a mercy from Us after some adversity that has visited him, he will surely say, ‘This is my due! I do not think the Hour will ever set in. And even if I am returned to my Lord, I will indeed have the best with Him.’ 41:50). This view would be acceptable were it not for the end of the verse and its upcoming meaning.

His saying: **لا جَرَمَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ النَّارَ وَأَنَّهُمْ مُفْرَطُونَ** (Undoubtedly, the Fire shall be their lot, and they shall be the first to be sent [into it]. 16:62). That is, those sent forward to the punishment of the Fire. It is said "he preceded" (فرط) and "he exceeded" (أفرط), meaning he went forward. Excess (الإفراط) is extravagance in going forward, just as negligence (التفريط) is falling short in it. The "forerunner" (الفرط) — *with two fatḥahs* — is the one who precedes the caravan to prepare the lodging and water. It is said: "he sent him forward" (أفرطه).

Since their saying—that Allah has what they dislike and they have the best—was a lie and fabrication in the sense of claiming they preceded their Lord to the best and left for Him what they dislike, He threatened them with the reality of this claim as a recompense for their lie. That is, the Fire is for them and they are truly sent forward to it. This is His saying: **لا جَرَمَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ النَّارَ** (Undoubtedly, the Fire shall be their lot) etc.

His saying, the Exalted: **تَاللَّهِ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا إِلَى أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ فَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمُ الْيَوْمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ** "By Allah, We certainly sent [apostles] to nations before you, but Satan made their deeds seem fair to them. So he is their patron today, and there is a painful punishment for them." (16:63). The apparent context suggests that "today" (اليوم) refers to the day the verse was revealed. The meaning of Satan being their patron (ولياً) on that day is their consensus upon deviation (الضلال) during the time of revelation. The promised punishment refers to the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, as is apparent from most verses that threaten with punishment.

The meaning is: By Allah, We certainly sent Our messengers to nations before you—such as the Jews, Christians, and Magians, among those who have not become extinct like ʿĀd and Thamūd—but Satan made their deeds seem fair to them, so they followed him and turned away from Our messengers. Thus, he is their patron today while they are united in deviation, and for them in the Day of Resurrection is a painful punishment.

Al-Zamakhshari allowed, based on this interpretation, that the pronoun in "their patron" (وليّهم) refers to Quraysh. The meaning would then be that Satan made the deeds of past nations seem fair to them, and he is today the patron of Quraysh. However, this is far-fetched as it necessitates a discrepancy in the referents of the pronouns.

It is also possible that "nations" refers to the past and perished nations; thus, Satan’s patronage over them "today" refers to them being among the partisans of Satan in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — and they have a painful punishment there.

It has also been said: "today" refers to the duration of this world, for it is the day of patronage, while the punishment is on the Day of Resurrection.

It has also been said: it refers to the Day of Resurrection; it is there that Satan is their patron and they have a painful punishment.

It has also been said: it refers to the day Satan made their deeds seem fair to them, which would be a case of narrating a past state (حكاية الحال الماضية).

The closest of these views is the first, followed by the next, and so on. And Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ إِلَّا لِتُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ الَّذِي اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ** "We did not send down the Book to you except [for the purpose] that you may clarify for them what they differ about..." (16:64). The pronoun "them" (لهم) refers to the polytheists (المشركين). "What they differ about" refers to the truth (الحق) regarding belief and action. Thus, "clarification" (التبين) means elucidation and disclosure to complete the proof (إتمام الحجة). The evidence for what we have mentioned is the distinction made for the affair of the believers and their separate mention in His saying: **وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ** "...and as a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith." (16:64).

The meaning is: This is the state of people regarding their differences in true knowledge and divine laws; We have not sent down the Book to you except to disclose to these disputants the truth about which they differed, so that the proof may be completed against them, and so it may be a guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — and a mercy (الرحمة) for a people who believe, whom Allah guides thereby to the truth and encompasses with mercy through faith and action.

## (Commentary on Narrations)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from ʿAbd al-Rahman ibn Kathir, who said: I said to Abu ʿAbdillah (peace be upon him) regarding: **فَاسْأَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** "Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know." (16:43). He said: **الذكر محمد ونحن أهله المسئولون** — "The Reminder (الذكر) is Muhammad, and we are his people who are to be questioned." (The hadith).

**I say:** He (peace be upon him) is referring to the saying of the Exalted: **قَدْ أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ إِلَيْكُمْ ذِكْرًا رَّسُولًا** "...Allah has already sent down to you a reminder, an apostle..." (65:10-11). There are many narrations with this meaning.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from al-Barqi, with his chain from ʿAbd al-Karim ibn Abi al-Daylam, from Abu ʿAbdillah (peace be upon him), who said: The Majestic in His mention said: **فَاسْأَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** "Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know." He said: **الكتاب الذكر وأهله آل محمد عليه السلام ـ أمر الله عز وجل بسؤالهم ولم يأمر بسؤال الجهال ـ وسمى الله عز وجل القرآن ذكرا فقال تبارك وتعالى وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ ـ وقال تعالى : وَإِنَّهُ لَذِكْرٌ لَكَ وَلِقَوْمِكَ وَسَوْفَ تُسْئَلُونَ** — "The Book is the Reminder (الذكر), and its people are the Progeny of Muhammad (peace be upon him). Allah, Mighty and Majestic, commanded that they be questioned and did not command the questioning of the ignorant. Allah, Mighty and Majestic, named the Quran a 'Reminder,' for the Blessed and Exalted said: 'We have sent down to you the Reminder that you may clarify for the people what has been sent down to them, and that they may reflect' (16:44). And the Exalted said: 'It is indeed a reminder for you and for your people, and you will be questioned' (43:44)."

**I say:** This is an argumentation for them being the "People of the Reminder" (أهل الذكر) based on the fact that the Reminder is the Quran, and they are its people because they are the people of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The two verses at the end of the speech serve as evidence for that, as explicitly stated in other narrations. There are other traditions with the same meaning as this hadith.

In *Tafsir al-ʿAyyashi*, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Jaʿfar (peace be upon him), who said: I said to him that some people among us claim that the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **فَاسْأَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** "Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know," refers to the Jews and Christians. He said: **إذا يدعونكم إلى دينهم** — "Then they would invite you to their religion." He then said, pointing with his hand to his chest: **نحن أهل الذكر ونحن المسئولون** — "We are the People of the Reminder, and we are the ones to be questioned." Abu Jaʿfar (peace be upon him) said: **الذكر القرآن** — "The Reminder is the Quran."

**I say:** A similar explanation was narrated from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) in the assembly of al-Maʾmun.

It has already been mentioned that the address in the verse, according to what the context provides, is to the polytheists (المشركين) among the idolaters who deemed the messengership impossible. They were commanded to ask the People of the Reminder—who are the people of the Heavenly Books: Did Allah ever send men from among mortals as messengers to whom He revealed? It is well known that since the polytheists did not accept from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), there would be no sense in referring them to others among the people of the Quran, for they did not acknowledge the Quran as a Reminder from Allah. Thus, it is determined that those to be questioned, in view of the specific occasion of the verse, are the People of the Book, especially the Jews.

However, if the saying: **فَاسْأَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** "Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know," is taken in itself, regardless of the specific occasion—and this is the nature of the Quran, as it is known that the occasion does not restrict the application—then the statement is general in terms of the asker, the one asked, and the subject of the question. The asker is anyone who may be ignorant of any of the true knowledge (المعارف) or legal issues (المسائل) among those burdened with religious duties (المكلفين). The subject of the question is all knowledge and issues that an ignorant person might not know. As for the one asked, although general in concept (المفهوم), they are specific in terms of the external referent (المصداق), and they are the People of the House (أهل البيت) of the Prophet (peace be upon him).

This is because if the "Reminder" (الذكر) refers to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as in the verse of Surah al-Talaq, then they are the People of the Reminder. If it refers to the Quran, as in the verse of Surah al-Zukhruf, then it is a reminder for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his people—and they are his people, or the most certain of his people—so they are its people and its elite, and they are the ones to be questioned. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) paired them with the Quran and commanded the people to hold fast to both in the mutawatir (متواتر) — *successively narrated and undeniably authentic* — Hadith of the Two Weighty Things (حديث الثقلين), saying: **إنهما لن يفترقا حتى يردا علي الحوض** — "Indeed, the two shall never separate until they return to me at the Pond." (The hadith).

Evidence that their speech (peace be upon them) stems from the perspective we mentioned is their lack of engagement with the specific details of the verse's occasion.

From what we have presented, the fallacy of the objection raised by some against these hadiths becomes clear—namely, that the polytheists who were commanded to ask would not accept from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), so how could they accept from his Household?

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: **لا ينبغي للعالم أن يسكت على علمه ، ولا ينبغي للجاهل أن يسكت على جهله وقد قال الله ـ فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ ـ فينبغي للمؤمن أن يعرف عمله على هدى أم على خلافه** — "It is not fitting for the scholar to remain silent regarding his knowledge, nor is it fitting for the ignorant to remain silent regarding his ignorance, for Allah has said: 'Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know.' Thus, it is fitting for the believer to know whether his action is based on guidance or its opposite."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **أَفَأَمِنَ الَّذِينَ مَكَرُوا السَّيِّئَاتِ** "Do those who devise evil schemes feel secure..." (16:45) until His saying: **بِمُعْجِزِينَ** "...[they cannot] frustrate [Him]?" (16:46). He (peace be upon him) said: **إذا جاءوا وذهبوا في التجارات فيأخذهم في تلك الحالة أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ عَلى تَخَوُّفٍ قال : قال : على تيقظ** — "When they come and go in trade, He seizes them in that state, 'or that He should seize them through gradual diminishment' (16:47)." He said: "Meaning, while they are alert (تيقظ)."

In *Tafsir al-ʿAyyashi*, from Samaʿah, from Abu ʿAbdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I asked about the saying of Allah: **وَلَهُ الدِّينُ وَاصِبًا** "...and to Him belongs the religion (الدين) — *the system of submission and worship* — constantly (واصباً)." (16:52). He said: **واجبا** — "Obligatorily (واجباً)."

In *al-Maʿani*, with its chain from Hannan ibn Sadir, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a hadith where he said: **وَلِلَّهِ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلَى** — "And to Allah belongs the highest attribute (المثل الأعلى) (16:60)—that which nothing resembles, and which cannot be described or imagined."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying: **وَلَوْ يُؤَاخِذُ اللَّهُ النَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِم** "Were Allah to take mankind to task for their wrongdoing..." (16:61). Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: **لو أن الله يؤاخذني وعيسى بن مريم بذنوبنا ـ وفي لفظ : بما جنت هاتان الإبهام والتي تليها ـ لعذبنا ما يظلمنا شيئا** — "If Allah were to take me and Jesus, son of Mary, to task for our sins—and in another wording: for what these two, the thumb and the one next to it, have committed—He would have punished us without wronging us in the least."

**I say:** This hadith contradicts what is established by the Book and the Sunnah regarding the infallibility (العصمة) — *the divine protection from error and sin* — of the Prophets (peace be upon them). There is no basis for interpreting it as referring to "leaving the better option" (ترك الأولى) among sins, for there is no punishment for that.
